+ Fantasy-humour: Hell is Other Demons — Score: 88.0
Prompt:
Hell is Other Demons Write a day in the life short story. Lucifer morningstar's on again/ off again relationship with Lilith is currently in the "off again" stage, though the cycle may be coming around as Lilith has been stopping by for reasons other than purely violent ones, and Lucifer has been increasingly lonely. Write a day in the life of this power couple, with elements of dark comedy & fantasy. Use understated irony & absurdity by merging the black immorality and everyday business of running hell, against relatable domestic relationship drama. Take cues from Neil Gaiman. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Lucifer Morningstar’s mornings began, as all good mornings should, with an existential scream. Not his, of course—he was strictly management these days—but the echoing roar of the Damned Alarm, a siren constructed from the lungs of particularly vocal opera critics. It went off at six-thirteen infernal standard time, just as the King of Hell finished trimming the last blackthorn from his impeccable goatee. Another day, another eternity. He materialized in the Hall of Infinite Clerks, a corridor so long even eternity muttered about overtime. Paperwork drifted like ash, suspension‑file imps fluttering overhead, filing forms about other forms having been misfiled. Lucifer signed requisitions for brimstone rations (low‑sulfur options for the asthmatics in Circle Seven), denied a junior demon’s request for a “motivational torture podcast,” and approved a two‑headed incubus’s maternity leave—both heads were expecting. It was, in other words, Tuesday. The trouble started, as it invariably did, with Lilith. She appeared without warning between one rubber‑stamp thud and the next, smelling of scorched sandalwood and New York City street vendor onions at three a.m. The first smell was traditional, the second purely recreational. Her boots were lacquered obsidian; her grin, weaponized nostalgia. Lucifer’s left eyebrow rose several millimeters, a seismic event noted by interns on clipboards. “Lucy,” Lilith purred, dangling a mortal‑world coffee. It came in a holiday cup—red, with snowflakes—already steaming despite the brimstone climate. “We’re not doing pet names,” Lucifer replied. “And you know we use hollowed skulls for mugs down here.” She lifted the cup, took a sip. “I’m supporting local businesses. Capitalism is its own circle of Hell; I like to shop at the source.” Her scorched leather purse—a battered thing that seemed to breathe when no one looked directly at it—clunked onto his desk. Trinkets spilled: a cheap plastic halo keychain (still glowing, warranty void if fallen), a New Jersey Turnpike toll receipt singed around the edges, and a snow globe containing a perfectly miniaturized Times Square forever stuck at New Year’s Eve, the confetti a constant gentle blizzard of expired wishes. Lucifer tried for nonchalance, but loneliness had sharp elbows. “Visiting for work,” he prompted, “or planning to redecorate my calendar again?” Last time, she’d crossed out February and replaced it with “Malice.” Lilith’s amber eyes flicked up. “Actually, I need a favor.” The phrase hung in the sulfurous air like an unclaimed prayer. He gestured grandly; sulfur sparks spelled out: I’m Listening. “A mortal soul slipped through customs,” she said. “Belongs to a hedge‑fund manager. I had…plans for him.” Her smile suggested those plans involved lemon juice, paper cuts, and earnest TED Talks on ethics. “But some bureaucratic mix‑up bumped him to Limbo. I thought you, being Mr. Middle‑Management of the Damned, could expedite retrieval.” Lucifer drummed crimson fingernails upon ancient mahogany. Limbo fell under Purgatory’s jurisdiction—technically a celestial gray area with alarming paperwork. Doing Lilith a favor risked alerting the Archangels’ auditors. Still, she was here, sipping coffee like she’d never stormed out in a swirl of bats six months ago. “All right,” he said. “But you owe me brunch.” “Done. I’ll bring pastries made of broken resolutions.” They took the Centrifugal Elevator, watched by a cohort of gossiping harpies. In the cramped car, Lilith’s purse growled softly; something inside tried to chew its way free. She nudged it with her boot. “Not now, darling.” Lucifer glanced sideways. “Dare I ask?” “Souvenir,” she said. “A pocket‑sized existential dread. Mortals give them away for free on subways these days.” The elevator spat them out at Limbo Annex, cubicles stretching into a fog of beige morality. Souls in grey cardigans queued politely, uncertain whether to apologize for existing. A cherub with a clip-on tie manned Reception, halo flickering like bad fluorescent. Lucifer produced his executive seal—an upside‑down corporate smiley face—and stamped the hedge‑fund manager’s paperwork with a flourish that smelled faintly of burnt hope. “Transfer authorized,” he announced. The cherub squinted. “We usually need Form L‑666 in triplicate.” “Check page two,” Lucifer replied. The cherub checked. Page two was, of course, blank, but a single sentence blazed across it in flaming cursive: GOD SIGNED IT, PROBABLY. The cherub decided bureaucracy wasn’t worth damnation twice and waved them through. They exited into an antechamber tiled in tasteful despair. The retrieved mortal, wearing a Patagonia vest, looked up from his phone. “Hi? Is this the VIP lounge?” Lilith’s smile could’ve cracked obsidian. “Sure,” she cooed. To Lucifer she whispered, “I’m thinking eternal telemarketing. He’ll be the one cold‑calling himself.” Lucifer’s mouth twitched—half smirk, half something warmer. “Creative. I’ll have the Gremlins of Corporate Jargon draft the script.” They escorted the mortal toward the updraft of shrieking souls that served as Hell’s express lane. On the way, Lucifer cleared his throat—an unnecessary affectation, but he liked props. “After brunch,” he said casually, “perhaps we could…discuss the state of the calendar?” Lilith arched a brow sharp enough to slice wallpaper. “We’re discussing months again? Last time you begged for June back.” “I miss June.” “I burned June.” “Exactly. Romantic ashes.” She laughed—not the bone‑rattling cackle of wrathful seductresses, but a brief, genuine sound that startled a passing wraith into forgetting its chains. “Fine,” she said. “But July stays obliterated. Too many fireworks. They clash with my purse.” The purse promptly belched a stray sparkler. They arrived at the Pit of Reception, where intake demons debated whether the mortal’s Patagonia vest counted as pre‑torment. Paperwork concluded, Lucifer snapped his fingers. A throne of obsidian inscribed with passive‑aggressive motivational quotes (“Hang in there, Or Else”) emerged beside a snack cart stocked with Soul‑Free Gluten Nuggets—Lucifer’s latest attempt at employee wellness. Brunch commenced. Lilith conjured pastries whose icing spelled TODAY I QUIT GYM, the dough tasting of broken Fitbit promises. Between bites, conversation tiptoed around subjects that once set the universe on fire: commitment, monogamy clauses, whose turn it was to feed Cerberus Junior. “So,” Lilith said at last, licking despair‑flavored frosting from a clawed fingertip, “are you lonely down here, Lucy?” He considered a moment. Somewhere, a sinner screamed the alphabet backwards for eternity; a demon union rep demanded ergonomic pitchforks; overhead, the ceiling dripped warm remorse. “Occasionally,” he admitted. “Management isolates. Occupational hazard.” Lilith’s hand drifted into her purse and surfaced with the glowing halo keychain. She spun it between them like a coin. “I’ve been topside collecting amusements,” she said. “But glitter fades.” She let the halo extinguish in her palm. “Hell’s stable, at least. Terrible, but reliable.” Lucifer studied her. The first woman, also the first rebel—he respected consistency in branding. “Perhaps,” he said, “we could draft a new calendar. Shared custody of the months. Alternating Sabbaths. Hell has HR forms for everything.” She snorted. “Your HR still thinks a Safe Space is a smaller torture chamber without spikes. But… maybe.” A pause. In the pause, infinite damnations unfolded, and a fledgling hope stretched, catlike, across the molten tiles. “Tell you what,” Lilith said, snapping the snow globe open. The tiny Times Square blizzard escaped, swirling into Hell’s scorching air, falling as fragile, hissing snowflakes that vanished before touching ground. “We’ll try again, on a trial basis. Ninety‑day review, standard diabolical probation.” Lucifer offered his hand. She took it, warm as a fresh sin. “Deal,” he said. “And Lilith?” “Yes, Lucy?” “You left something in the elevator.” He produced the pocket‑sized existential dread. It trembled like a guilty pug. “Consider it re‑gifted.” She slid it back into her purse, which sighed contentedly. They parted at the fork where managerial duties diverged from recreational destruction. Lucifer headed to Circle Eight for a budget meeting on pitchfork maintenance. Lilith strolled toward the arena, where she planned to test out an experimental scream amplifier shaped like a karaoke machine. As they walked away, two demons gossiping near the cappuccino cauldron exchanged knowing glances. Relationships in Hell, they mused, were like the damned subscription services mortals forgot to cancel—inefficient, eternal, and always renewing at the worst possible moment. But then again, inefficiency was half the fun.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully blending dark comedy with fantasy elements while exploring the relationship dynamics between Lucifer and Lilith. The author effectively captures a Neil Gaiman-esque tone through understated irony and absurdist juxtapositions of cosmic evil with mundane bureaucracy. The characterization is particularly strong. Lucifer emerges as a weary middle manager dealing with the tedium of eternal administration, while Lilith maintains her rebellious independence. Their dialogue feels authentic to their mythological status while remaining grounded in relatable relationship dynamics. The author avoids making either character a simple archetype, instead presenting them as complex beings with genuine emotional needs. The prose style is sophisticated and inventive, with memorable phrases like "weaponized nostalgia" and "hollowed skulls for mugs." The imagery consistently supports the absurdist tone, from the "Damned Alarm" constructed from opera critics' lungs to the pocket-sized existential dread that behaves like a pet. The bureaucratic elements of Hell are particularly well-realized, creating humor through the mundane horror of eternal paperwork. The story maintains excellent pacing and structure, moving from Lucifer's morning routine through the favor request to their tentative reconciliation. The emotional arc feels earned rather than forced, with both characters acknowledging their loneliness and the appeal of their dysfunctional but familiar dynamic. The dialogue sparkles with wit and character-specific voice. Each exchange reveals personality while advancing both plot and relationship development. The banter feels natural despite the supernatural context, and the emotional beats land effectively without becoming overwrought. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the inventive language borders on being too clever for its own good, though this rarely detracts from the overall effect. The ending strikes an appropriate balance between hope and cynicism, avoiding unearned positivity while allowing for genuine character growth. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18

+ Historical fiction: Gladiator — Score: 86.2
Prompt:
Historical Fiction: Write a scene from a story set during the height of the Roman Empire, focusing on a slice of a day in the life of a gladiator. Do not write a combat scene. Use sensory details to capture the sights, sounds, and smells of ancient Rome. Explore the gladiator's thoughts and emotions. The story should also touch on the larger political and social issues of the time period. The piece should feel like a slice of a larger story. First person, past tense, 1000 words.
Model Output:
The clang of the iron-barred gate woke me long before the sun cleared the tiled roofs of the Ludus Magnus. Dawn’s first light filtered through the slit of my cell, painting the straw on which I lay a bruised violet. The air was already thick with the layered smells of the barracks: damp limestone, stale sweat, olive‑oil smoke from the torches that had burned all night, and the sour tang of the vinegar we drank to scrub yesterday’s dust from our throats. I rose, every scar on my back tightening like a stitch pulled too hard, and pressed my forehead to the wall—my morning ritual, the first part of it, anyway. A heartbeat’s communion with the stone to remind myself I was still flesh and not yet a ghost. Out in the corridor, wooden sandals slapped in uneven rhythms as slaves hurried toward the kitchen hearth. A pot of farro porridge hissed somewhere, and the scent of boiling grains mingled with garlic and pork fat. My stomach growled, but I waited. Three measured breaths, then I reached under the folded blanket on my cot for the amulet: a tiny copper figurine of Fortuna, battered smooth by years of handling. I kissed her feet—always the feet—then touched the figurine to each eyelid. The old Thracian who trained me claimed that Fortuna turned her wheel more gently for a man who acknowledged the mud before the crown. Superstition, yes, but so was half the Empire: augurs scanning birdflight, patricians reading burnt livers, emperors deified while their bodies were still warm. When I finally stepped into the courtyard, the sky was the color of a freshly honed blade, cloudless and hard. Forty of us gathered in loose ranks, some still yawning, others already rolling shoulders, the morning chill trapped in the steam of their breath. The sand underfoot clung to my soles, a reminder of where we would spend our lives—and most likely lose them. From across the yard I could hear the city already stirring. Mule carts trundled along the Vicus Tuscus, hawkers called out figs and honeyed nuts, and somewhere a woman scolded a child in shrill Oscan. Rome never truly slept; it only shifted its weight, like a beast dreaming of conquest. Our lanista, Cassius Pollio, appeared on the balcony above, toga as white as the marble he wished his lineage matched. He surveyed us the way a money‑changer weighs silver, lips pursed, brow furrowed as though the small arena beneath his house were the Senate floor. Rumor said Pollio had bought his way out of a provincial fraud charge by relinquishing a fortune to the emperor’s newest tax on fortunes. Now he filled his coffers by feeding men like me to the crowd’s appetite. Bread for the bellies, blood for the eyes—that was the bargain Rome struck with itself each morning. Training began with the wooden swords, the rudis. The thock of oak against oak echoed between stone walls, a percussion line under the cawing of rooftop crows. Sweat burned the cuts on my knuckles, but the ache was familiar, almost comforting. Across from me, Marcus the Syrian grinned through broken teeth, taunting in Greek, “Does the Gaul still dream of fields?” I answered with a low sweep meant to sting his shins. He dodged, laughing. We were friends, or as close as men can be when both know they might be ordered to kill each other before the month’s end. Between drills, Pollio read aloud the imperial bulletin. “His Excellency Trajan,” he began—stretching the name so we could taste its power—“has secured another victory across the Danube. The Senate proclaims one hundred days of celebration.” Cheers erupted; a hundred days meant a hundred games, and the more games, the higher the chance some of us would touch the wooden sword of freedom. Yet freedom bought with novelty was fragile. I had seen liberti begging outside the Subura, manumission tablets hanging from their necks like mockery. At midday we paused for the meal. Bowls of lentils, crusts of bread sprinkled with salt from Ostia’s flats. I ate seated on a low step, the sun now glaring from directly overhead. Its heat baked the sand, releasing the metallic scent of iron filings spilt during weapon repairs. A cicada droned somewhere near the barrack wall, its song a thin thread tying this day to every other long Roman summer. For a moment, memory slipped its leash: I saw green hills, felt the wet chill of a northern river on my calves, heard my sister’s voice carrying over barley stalks. The vision hurt more than any blade. But when Marcus plopped beside me, laughter in his eyes, the dream scattered like sparrows from a cat. We spent the afternoon oiling armor. The segmented plates of my lorica glinted, each ridge catching sun and reflecting it onto the wall in trembling patches. Ravens swooped overhead, drawn by the promise of scraps after tomorrow’s spectacles. I counted them—another piece of my ritual. If their number was even, Fortuna smiled. Seven today. I spat on the ground twice, then a third time—never fewer than three—and rubbed the saliva with my heel. Spit to bind, heel to hide; so taught the Thracian. Marcus rolled his eyes. “The gods are busy, friend,” he said. “They care little for spit.” I shrugged. “Then let them ignore me one more day.” Late afternoon brought a visit from the domina, Pollio’s wife, Livia Severina. She drifted through the yard in silk the hue of crushed pomegranates, attendant slaves shading her with fans of peacock feathers. Perfume of myrrh and rose preceded her, briefly conquering the stench of sweat. She paused to examine us like livestock, fingers brushing the ridges of my collarbone. “This one will entertain,” she told her steward, voice cool as well‑water. “Put him after the executions but before the chariot races; the crowd will be hungry for a closer kill.” My gut tightened—not fear of the fight, but of being slotted like a tile in someone else’s mosaic. I bowed my head, not trusting my voice. When she left, the yard exhaled. Marcus whispered, “Better her hand than the emperor’s.” He referred to the rumor sliding through Rome like grease: conspirators in the Senate planned to test Trajan’s patience. More games meant more taxes; more taxes meant more knives sharpened in shadows. The city felt ready to crack, though marble seemed unbreakable until it split. As the sun dipped toward the Aventine, we were finally dismissed. I returned to my cell, passing under the arch where previous champions’ names were carved. Most had dates beside them; a few were blank, the stone waiting for an end it knew would come. Inside, I knelt on the straw. Outside, the city began its evening chorus: tavern doors creaking open, lyres plucking from balconies, sandals scuffing mosaics. Somewhere near the Forum, senators would be reclining at banquets, debating provinces they would never see. I rinsed my hands in a wooden basin, water gone tepid. Then I opened the tiny leather pouch hanging from my belt and took out the second half of my ritual: a strip of cloth torn from my father’s cloak the day the legionaries dragged me from our farm. I wrapped it around my left wrist, knotting it twice—twice, never once—while murmuring a fragment of his favorite harvest hymn. The words were Gallic, foreign on Roman soil, but soft in my mouth. Night settled. Torches sputtered in the corridor, scenting the air with resin. I lay on the straw again, Fortune’s amulet cool against my chest, and listened to the distant rumble of the Colosseum being cleaned for tomorrow. Scrapers on stone, buckets sloshing water tinted pink from today’s blood. Rome would wake to find the arena pristine, as if violence were a dream washed away with dawn. I knew better. I carried every scream I had ever caused, every friend I might yet lose. But I also carried the weight of seven ravens and a strip of cloth and a goddess’s worn copper feet. For a man whose fate was decided by others, these small reliquiae were a rebellion—my own Senate, my own empire, ruled by the single law that I must survive one more day.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated piece of historical fiction that demonstrates strong craft and attention to detail. The author successfully creates an immersive Roman setting through carefully chosen sensory details - the "bruised violet" dawn light, the layered smells of the barracks, the metallic scent of iron filings in heated sand. The first-person narration maintains a consistent voice throughout, balancing the protagonist's immediate physical reality with deeper emotional and philosophical reflections. The characterization is particularly strong. The gladiator narrator emerges as a complex figure - neither a stereotypical noble savage nor a broken victim, but a man who has developed intricate personal rituals and coping mechanisms. His relationship with Marcus the Syrian feels authentic, capturing the paradox of friendship among men who might be forced to kill each other. The protagonist's memories of his Gallic homeland are woven in naturally without overwhelming the present narrative. The prose quality is generally excellent, with vivid imagery that avoids excessive ornamentation. Phrases like "Rome never truly slept; it only shifted its weight, like a beast dreaming of conquest" demonstrate skillful metaphorical thinking. The author effectively uses concrete details to suggest larger themes - the domina examining gladiators like livestock, the senators debating provinces they'll never see. The piece successfully incorporates period-appropriate political and social commentary without feeling forced. References to Trajan's campaigns, tax burdens, and potential Senate conspiracies feel organic to the world. The exploration of superstition and ritual across all social classes adds depth to the historical setting. However, there are some areas where the writing occasionally becomes slightly overwrought. Certain passages, particularly some of the more philosophical reflections, border on purple prose. The ending, while thematically appropriate, feels somewhat heavy-handed in its symbolism about personal agency and rebellion. The piece adheres well to the prompt requirements - it's clearly set during the Roman Empire's height, focuses on a non-combat day, incorporates rich sensory details, and feels like part of a larger narrative. The emotional complexity is genuine, avoiding both sentimentality and excessive darkness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 7 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 6.0, Overwrought: 7.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 1.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Epistolary: The Lighthouse Keeper — Score: 79.1
Prompt:
The Lighthouse Keeper Epistolary Narrative: Write a story told entirely through a series of brief correspondences: telegrams or letters or emails between two characters. The correspondence should span several months or years, and reveal a gradually unfolding plot. Use distinct voices for each character, and include details that provide insight into their personalities and motivations. The story should build to an emotional climax, and the final letter should provide a satisfying resolution. The setting is a lighthouse keeper writing to his mother. He is working class and Scottish. He is struggling with the isolation of his posting. Write naturally and without cliches. 1000 words.
Model Output:
1. 17 March 2021 Lighthouse Cottage, Eilean Sgeir Ma, Made landfall yesterday after three hours in a boat that kicked like a bull. The tower is chipped but standing, same as in the pamphlet. My room’s no bigger than the pantry back home, yet the view swallows you whole. I’ve chalked the maintenance rota on the wall: lamp at 0400, fuel check at 0800, weather log every four hours. Seems simple enough—aye, that’s what they all say first week. Your son, Calum — 2. 23 March 2021 Dennistoun, Glasgow Dear Calum, I wiped down the windows this morning and could almost feel your salt wind. Mrs. Ahmed from next door sends a tin of almond biscuits (I’ll post them when the ferry runs). Don’t forget the liniment for your knee. Your father’s old thermos is on the hall table; I like to think it remembers the North Sea too. Write when you can—the house is too quiet without the kettle whistling over your midnight toast. Love, Ma — 3. 14 April 2021 Ma, First proper gale last night. The whole tower shuddered like a tuning fork; I wedged myself under the stairs till it passed. Discovered the generator only likes to start if I swear at it in Gaelic—whatever works. Lonelier than I expected. The radio brings in nothing but trawler crackle and the shipping forecast, each voice gone before I can answer. Still, I’m keeping the light steady. That matters. Calum — 4. 30 April 2021 Son, Mrs. McBride from the bakery gave me day‑old rolls, so I made bread pudding and thought of you spooning it straight from the dish. Isolation’s a hard teacher, but she’ll show you what you’re made of. Remember: talk out loud even if no one’s there. Your gran used to say words keep the dark from congealing. I’m knitting you a hat the colour of heather—impossible to lose against the sea. Proud of you, Ma — 5. 12 June 2021 Ma, Mid‑summer but the fog sits thick as porridge. Supply boat is delayed again; tins are down to beans and peaches. Worst is the silence after the lamp’s wound and I’ve nothing but my own breathing. Found a gull chick trapped in the gutter—named him Stovies. He screams for fish at sunrise, gives the place a bit of company. Don’t laugh. Calum — 6. 8 July 2021 Dear Calum, A gull’s cheaper than a dog, so carry on. Your cousin Jamie phoned—he’s signed on with the Coastguard out of Oban. Says the Western Isles need keepers with shore leave; might be work nearer civilisation. No pressure, just options. Eat the peaches, not only the beans. Sugar keeps spirits up. All my love, Ma — 7. 3 October 2021 Ma, Bad news: the diesel tank has a hairline crack. I caught it early, but every night I taste fumes in my dreams. Headquarters say a mechanic will come “weather permitting.” Forecast says storms for a fortnight. If the light fails I’ll have to run the backup lamp by hand—fifteen‑minute turns on the crank. I’m writing this now in case the mail boat can still dock tomorrow. Calum — 8. 15 October 2021 Son, The mechanic’s still not there? I rang the office till someone answered; they promised next tide. Calum, ask for help before the help is too late. Your father once worked a rig where the gas alarms were always “next tide”—he nearly didn’t come home. You don’t have to prove anything to anyone, least of all me. Holding you in my thoughts, Ma — 9. 1 November 2021 Ma, Took a blow to the hand fixing the crank—split two knuckles, nothing serious. But on the worst night the lamp died, and for twenty minutes the headland was dark. I kept thinking of cargo ships, lives I’d never meet. Got it burning again, though the glass is scorched. I haven’t slept since. Stovies flew off yesterday; smart bird. Maybe he sensed I’m no good company. Calum — 10. 18 November 2021 My dear boy, The biscuits you sent arrived in crumbs, but I ate every one. Listen: when your dad passed, I spent evenings staring at the mantel clock, convinced if I kept it wound I could hold everything together. It took Mrs. Ahmed banging the door down with a casserole to show me I was breaking, not mending. Quit the post if you must. A job is only a job; a son is irreplaceable. Come home for Christmas? Love, Ma — 11. 6 January 2022 Port Office, Oban Ma, Handed in my keys on Hogmanay. The relief keeper shook my bandaged hand and said the glass would polish out. I told him the crack in the diesel tank was fixed; I didn’t mention the crack in my head. Jamie found me berth in the harbourmaster’s office—tide tables, ferry schedules, people’s voices humming like bees. I’m still hearing phantom waves at night, but they’re getting softer. See you next week. Put the kettle on. Your wandering lad, Calum — 12. 14 January 2022 Dennistoun, Glasgow Welcome home, Calum, Your room is smaller than you remember because every shelf holds something you sent: a shell, a feather, a stone rubbed flat by the sea. I won’t dust them; they’ve earned their salt. When you’re ready we’ll visit Eilean Sgeir together, stand on the pier, and let you look without holding the whole horizon alone. Till then, there’s bread pudding on the stove, and a hat the colour of heather waiting by your chair. Always, Ma
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This epistolary narrative successfully fulfills most of the prompt requirements, creating a compelling story through correspondence between Calum, a Scottish lighthouse keeper, and his mother. The piece spans nearly a year, tracking Calum's psychological deterioration in isolation and his eventual decision to leave his post. The characterization is particularly strong. Calum's voice feels authentically working-class Scottish without resorting to heavy dialect or stereotypes. His letters reveal a man trying to maintain stoicism while gradually cracking under isolation's pressure. The mother's voice is distinct—warm, practical, and increasingly concerned. Her advice feels genuine rather than saccharine, particularly her story about the mantel clock and her direct urging for him to quit if necessary. The story structure builds tension effectively through escalating crises: the gale, equipment failures, the diesel leak, and finally the lamp going dark. The detail about twenty minutes of darkness and Calum thinking of "cargo ships, lives I'd never meet" powerfully conveys his sense of responsibility and guilt. The pet gull Stovies serves as an effective metaphor for companionship and abandonment. The prose is generally elegant and restrained, avoiding purple language while maintaining poetic touches like "fog sits thick as porridge" and "words keep the dark from congealing." The imagery effectively conveys the harsh maritime environment and psychological isolation. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The resolution feels somewhat rushed—Calum's decision to leave happens between letters with little exploration of that crucial moment. The final exchange, while touching, edges toward sentimentality that doesn't quite feel earned given the psychological damage suggested earlier. The mother's final letter, with its poetic language about shells and stones, feels slightly incongruent with her previously practical voice. The story also relies on some familiar beats of isolation narratives, though it executes them competently. The injured hand and equipment failures are somewhat predictable plot points, though they serve the emotional arc well. + First-person unique perspective: Hacker Mindset — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Hacker Mindset Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a reformed (now) white hat hacker who cannot help but notice exploitable weaknesses in both systems and people they encounter. They habitually look for vulnerabilities and exploit vectors. The narrative takes place during a first date at a restaurant, where they're trying to focus on human connection. It's going surprisingly well due to a discovered shared nerd-fandom (magic the gathering). Things go a little sideways when the date reveals a noticeably insecure phone unlock pattern, which protagonist can't help but comment on. They are then left to negotiate the fallout of this faux pas and attempt to salvage (or perhaps sabotage) the remainder of the date. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
I step through the vestibule of The Peach Bistro and, before my coat is even off, the reconnaissance loop in my skull starts running. POS station on the hostess stand: Windows 7 sticker, long‑unsupported. Wi‑Fi SSID scrolling on a tiny marquee behind the bar: PeachBistroGuest, WPA2 but the password—“freshpeach”—is printed right on the laminated dessert menu. Somebody’s already set up a rogue access point; I can see the slightly hotter signal in my phone’s list. Interesting, but tonight is about Alex, not triaging a stranger’s network. I exhale, push the packets out of my mind, and walk toward the two‑top by the window where they’re waiting. Alex stands as I approach—Owlbear T‑shirt, denim jacket covered in enamel pins, one of which is clearly a tiny Jace Beleren silhouette. Good sign. I shake their hand. Eye contact: full second and a half, comfortable. Connection request acknowledged. We start talking—small stuff first—and I force myself to listen instead of map attack surfaces. I notice, though, that Alex’s smartwatch is running an old Wear OS build, probably still vulnerable to that Bluetooth stack overflow from last year. Focus, I tell myself, and drop the thought into mental cold storage. Menus open; conversation opens wider. The moment Magic: The Gathering surfaces, my threat‑assessment daemon goes quiet, replaced by the humming server fans of enthusiasm. Alex mentions, casually, “I’ve been tinkering with Izzet recently—thinking about slotting Crackling Drake back in.” Catalog entry begins: Izzet, spellslinger leaning. Stored card list: Crackling Drake (CMC 4, P/T based on instants + sorceries). Sub‑archetype guess: Tempo‑burn. I respond with a story about misplaying a Niv‑Mizzet, Parun trigger at last month’s FNM; their laugh is genuine, bright. We trade card anecdotes like old speakeasy passwords. Alex shares: “I still love throwing Expansion // Explosion at people who forget I have six untapped lands.” Mental catalog: Expansion // Explosion, indicates preference for flexible X‑spells, closers that double as copy spells. Noted. Food arrives—fried green tomatoes for them, mushroom risotto for me. I study the silverware: standardized, stamped, magnet adheres (so not cheap fake stainless). I am analyzing cutlery hardness now; I need a brain firewall. We’re halfway through entrées when Alex pulls out their phone to show me a photo of a cosplay version of Chandra they did. OLED screen, Samsung, a few generations old. They swipe a quick “L” pattern—top left, bottom left, bottom right—to unlock. My lizard brain whispers 1624 possible combinations, easily brute‑forced in minutes if smudge patterns reveal start node. I let the sentence slip out before the socialization module can quarantine it. “You know, anyone who watches you do that once could unlock your phone.” The words hit the table like a dropped fork. Alex’s finger freezes above the touchscreen. Their eyes flick up—blue, widening. I might as well have told them their fly was down while detailing the zipper’s material weakness. Internal sirens: Faux Pas Detected. Initiate damage control. But the part of me that used to live in IRC chatrooms at 3 a.m. cannot stop elaborating. “It’s just that the ‘L’ is one of the most common patterns. Smudges give it away, and—” Abort, abort. Alex sets the phone beside their plate—screen dark now—like it’s something newly suspicious. Their shoulders have retreated an inch. Great. Ten minutes ago we were bonding over the elegance of Lightning Bolt; now I’m the weird security evangelist. I rewind the last thirty seconds in my head, looking for a salvage vector. Honesty patch incoming. “I’m sorry,” I say—out loud, though the rest remains in thought. “Occupational hazard.” I wait. Alex takes a sip of water, measuring me. I fill the silence internally: Explain or retreat? If I explain, I disclose the past. White hat work is respectable, but ex‑black‑hat origin story can spook people. However, vulnerability disclosure is only useful when complete, right? Okay. Go full responsible disclosure. “My brain notices patterns,” I add. “I used to be on the, uh, darker side of that. Now I get paid to make things safer, but the reflex is hardwired.” Alex’s eyebrows unknot a millimeter; they’ve heard of pen‑testing at least. Opportunity window reopens, 50% width. I push: “I shouldn’t have jumped in unasked. Force of habit. I promise I was listening to the Chandra story, not casing your phone.” They examine me like a judge deciding whether intent mitigates crime. Finally a small smile: “So you’re saying my unlock pattern is basically a vanilla 1/1?” I exhale—a laugh disguised as relief. “More like a 0/1 Goat token.” Smile widens. Crisis mostly averted. We reroute. They swipe again to show me the cosplay photo. I keep my eyes on the costume, not the pattern. Chandra goggles, flame aura Photoshopped—impressive. Alex is telling me how they wired orange LEDs into the gloves. Catalog update: Creativity high, enjoys hardware tinkering. Possible synergy. The server appears with dessert menus. Alex says, “You pick.” Trust restored enough to outsource decision; that feels important. I scan dessert list—and the restaurant’s backend again: tablets running Square, still on default admin passwords if sticker codes are untouched. I suppress another comment. We split a peach cobbler. Between bites, deck theory resumes. Alex mentions Arclight Phoenix. I note: graveyard recursion via spell volume. Their deck is shaping into the classic Izzet Arclight shell. They confess, “I still can’t decide if I want to include Treasure Cruise even after the ban in other formats. Feels greedy.” Catalog: Card draw priorities, willingness to flirt with banned tech. Personality marker: risk‑aware yet tempted—like me, once. Conversation drifts to upcoming prerelease. I imagine us shoulder to shoulder at the LGS, sideboarding together. My heart frames it as a threat model: probability of second date escalating to public appearance. Acceptable risk. Bill arrives—it’s the little leather folio with the mag‑stripe reader built in. I’d bet the restaurant stores card data improperly. My mental fingers itch to verify, to help them fix it, to protect every diner here. Instead I slip in my chip card. Restraint: the unsung patch. I glance at Alex’s phone again, perched by the water glass. I consider offering to show them stronger authentication. But the memory of their earlier flinch restrains me. They’ll ask if they want advice; unsolicited pen‑testing is just hacking by another name. Still, I can’t help performing a silent calculation: nine‑node grid, begin at 1, connect 4,7,8—creates a zigzag that’s harder to mimic. I store it for later if they request. We stand. Coats on. Outside the door, night air. Streetlights halo off wet pavement—perfect reflections for shoulder‑surfing, my brain notes. Ignore. Alex stuffs hands in pockets. “So, prerelease next Saturday? I could use a sparring partner.” That’s an invitation. I weigh outcomes: 70% chance of additional vulnerability disclosures, 90% chance of enjoying myself. Accept. “Deal,” I say, and our handshakes linger just long enough to call it a promise. As we part, I glimpse the corner of their phone screen: they’ve changed the unlock pattern—can’t tell to what, only that it’s longer. I smile at the unspoken patch note, proud and humbled at once. Walking toward my car, I finally release the buffer of system flaws I’ve been suppressing: the outdated POS, the rogue AP, the Square reader’s poor encryption. They cascade through my thoughts like an audit report. But for once they feel like background noise, not the whole symphony. Because tonight, the most interesting system I probed wasn’t a device or a network—it was the delicate negotiation of trust between two people, and that, I decide, is one surface I’m willing to explore at minimum privilege, one gentle ping at a time.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative interpretation of the prompt. The author successfully captures the hacker mindset through consistent use of technical terminology and thought patterns that feel authentic - from "reconnaissance loop" to "damage control" to thinking in terms of "attack surfaces" and "vulnerability disclosure." The internal monologue format is well-maintained throughout, with the protagonist's thoughts seamlessly blending cybersecurity concepts with social interaction. The Magic: The Gathering integration is particularly well-executed, serving both as authentic shared interest and clever metaphorical framework. The author demonstrates genuine knowledge of the game, using specific card names and mechanics that enhance rather than distract from the narrative. The protagonist's tendency to "catalog" information about Alex's deck preferences mirrors their security assessment habits effectively. The central conflict - the phone unlock pattern observation - feels organic and believable. The protagonist's inability to suppress their professional instincts creates genuine tension, and their immediate recognition of the social misstep adds authenticity. The recovery attempt through honest disclosure about their background shows character growth and emotional intelligence. However, the piece occasionally veers toward technical showing-off, with some cybersecurity references feeling slightly forced or overly detailed for the narrative context. The "mental catalog" entries, while clever, sometimes interrupt the flow. The resolution, while satisfying, comes perhaps too easily - Alex's quick forgiveness and immediate pattern change feels slightly convenient. The prose style is generally strong, with creative metaphors that blend the digital and social worlds effectively. The ending strikes an appropriate balance between hope and realism, avoiding both cynicism and unearned optimism. The final reflection on trust as a system to be explored "at minimum privilege" provides a satisfying thematic conclusion that ties the technical and emotional elements together. The characterization feels nuanced, particularly in showing the protagonist's struggle between their ingrained analytical habits and desire for genuine human connection. Alex emerges as more than just a plot device, with their own interests and reasonable reactions to the protagonist's behavior. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 4 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ First-person unique perspective: Overthinking It — Score: 88.4
Prompt:
Overthinking It Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety who has developed a crush on a classmate. The narrative takes place during a group science project where they're paired together, as she cycles through rehearsed conversations while second-guessing each potential approach. Her inner thoughts should reveal the gap between her articulate internal voice and her difficulty expressing herself aloud, her fears of coming across as desperate or weird, and the exhausting process of monitoring her every word, expression, and movement for potential embarrassment. Write almost entirely in internal monologue. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Mrs. Hirasawa says, “You’ll have the rest of the period to plan your demonstration,” and my stomach flips so hard I half‑expect it to knock over the graduated cylinders. Demonstration equals partners, partners equals small talk, small talk equals me freezing up like an overheated laptop. I pretend to adjust the focus on the microscope—nothing under the lens, it’s just glass—while she reads out the groups. “And finally: Evan, Tara, Mia, and Joel.” Evan. That’s him. The boy who wears untied sneakers and knows the Latin names of every tree on campus. I’ve learned more botanical trivia eavesdropping on his conversations than from the entire chapter we were assigned. Also: the way the right corner of his mouth lifts before he laughs, like a comma waiting for the next clause. Also: his handwriting slants left, an introvert tilt. I’ve cataloged these facts like pressed leaves, neat and hidden. Okay, Tara and Mia are in the group, too—safety buffers. I can handle this. I think. Rehearsal Track #1: “Hi, Evan, I was thinking we could compare photosynthesis rates under different light spectra. We could use the color gels from theater club.” Response Imagination #1: He says, “Cool idea,” smiles, thinks I’m competent. Anxiety add‑on: My voice cracks, he hears “Hey, oven,” not “Evan,” wonders if I’m making an appliance joke, decides I’m bizarre. Abort Track #1. I open my notebook to a blank page and, with my mechanical pencil, draw a four‑petaled flower the size of a dime: that’s me, petals tight, stem curved like a shy back. Beside it I sketch a taller sunflower—Evan—slightly leaning toward the little bloom because physics says light bends. Tara becomes a hummingbird (she never stops moving), Mia a cloud with earbuds (always drifting, always music). Tiny ecosystem of our group, safely two‑dimensional. Tara bounces over first. “So, what’re we doing? Volcano? Too cliché? What about dry ice bubbles?” She’s spinning an unused pipette like a baton. Rehearsal Track #2: Compliment her enthusiasm, propose a plan, delegate tasks. Problem: Need vocal cords. They appear stuck in airplane‑mode. I nod—great, thrilling nod! She takes it as consent and starts googling dry ice suppliers. Crisis postponed, but not solved. Evan slides onto the stool across from me. My pulse jumps so high I swear it taps the underside of my chin. He says, very normally, “Hey, is this your notebook?” His fingers brush the corner. He’s about to see the doodles. Abort mission? Too late. Words, I need words. My mouth opens but releases only a half vowel, like a phone waking up with no Wi‑Fi. I slam it shut. My brain, meanwhile, drafts a thesis on Why Showing Him the Sketch Is Both Terrifying and Potentially Endearing, complete with footnotes. He’s already looking. “These are cool. Did you draw them just now?” Internal fireworks: He noticed! He likes them! He’s staring! He Wrote ‘cool’! Wait, he’ll figure out who the sunflower is. He’ll think I’m a stalker. Worse, he’ll pity me. Emergency Strategy: Play it off as random. I shrug. “Just... bored.” Two syllables escape, small, shaky, like escaped lab mice. Immediate Post‑mortem: “Just bored”? He compliments my art and I act indifferent? He’ll assume I don’t care about his opinion. Why am I like this? I add a new doodle: a speech bubble with legs, sprinting away from a stick figure version of me. Mia arrives last, earbud wires curling from her pocket. She offers a sleepy wave. Good, group assembled. Consensus time. Tara’s halfway into an online tutorial about sublimation fog. Evan scrolls on his tablet, probably researching the same thing, maybe thinking of ways to integrate chlorophyll data because he never stays surface‑level. I could connect the chemistry and the biology, thread the two like double helix strands, impress him. The words exist inside me, lined up like soldiers. I just have to open the gate. Rehearsal Track #3: “If we introduce plant leaves to the carbon‑dioxide‑rich vapor, we can show stomatal response under temperature stress.” Potential Reaction: Everyone nods, we become the dream team, we win the science fair, Evan asks for my number to send data—strictly data, but numbers can evolve. Anxiety Counterargument: I mispronounce ‘stomatal,’ say ‘stom‑a‑tul,’ class laughs, Tara covers but Evan’s secondhand embarrassment skyrockets. Meanwhile, real time is passing. Tara says, “So, Mia and I can handle supplies. Evan, you good with explaining the reaction part?” He glances at me. “What about Sadie?” (Me. He said my name. Mark the timestamp.) All eyes pivot. Spotlights. My brain flips cue cards: You are prepared. Speak. I manage, “I—uh—could do the plant angle?” It comes out like a question pitched too high, voice cracking on ‘plant.’ Mortification level: lava. Tara beams. “Perfect!” She doesn’t notice the crack; maybe it sounded normal to non‑paranoid ears. Evan nods—casual, approving. I’m still alive. Remarkable. Still, the autopsy begins: volume? pitch? Did my eyebrows do that weird slant they do? Did my hands clutch the pencil too hard? I uncurl my fingers; graphite smudge tattoos my palm. The period ticks forward. We divide tasks. I scribble bullet points but my attention keeps detouring to Evan’s profile—the way his hair falls over one eye until he blows it aside, exactly every forty seconds (I counted). I imagine saying, “You know you could pin it back while you work,” handing him a paperclip. Could be friendly, helpful. Or meddling, weird. Compute pathways: Track #4A (friendly): He laughs, bends the clip, says thanks, our fingertips brush, micro‑electricity. Track #4B (weird): He thinks I’m policing his hair, files me under Controlling People, avoids future interaction. Statistical outcome: unknown. Decision: stay silent, shred idea. I draw a tiny paperclip with a question mark inside it, then box it in caution tape. Mrs. Hirasawa circulates, praises our “diverse approach.” I give a polite smile, upper teeth only. Note to self: lower teeth exposure risks spinach detection (even though I haven’t eaten spinach today, probability remains non‑zero). Ten minutes left. I fill our planning sheet with vocabulary so advanced the others might Google them later. I know it’s overcompensation, but if I can’t talk smoothly, at least my written words can swagger. Evan peers over. “You spelled ‘spectrophotometric’ right on the first try?” He sounds almost awed. My cheeks heat—pleasure plus panic. Rehearsal Track #5: Humble response, maybe a joke. “I collect prefixes for fun.” Counter‑fear: Braggy? Nerdy? Both? Actual output: “Um, autocorrect?” Stupid. Pencil and paper don’t have autocorrect. He’ll notice. He laughs, gentle, like it’s obviously a joke. Thank every known deity. Bell rings. Chaos erupts: stools scraping, backpacks zipping. My whole body sags, batteries drained. Tara high‑fives the air between us—misses, tries again, lands a soft slap on my palm. Mia half‑smiles, earbud reinstalling. Evan gathers his tablet, then pauses. “See you tomorrow, Sadie.” Four straightforward words. Responses Tested: A) “See you!” (safe) B) “Looking forward to it!” (too eager) C) “Later.” (cool, but maybe cold) I shoot for A but panic mid‑launch, mash them. “See—later!” It comes out like a new verb. Sealater. Great. I’ve invented marine‑themed slang. He’s already walking away, but he gives a thumbs‑up. Interpretations: 1) He didn’t notice, 2) He’s politely ignoring, 3) He thinks it’s quirky‑cute. Schrödinger’s thumb. I exhale so hard my bangs lift. Close notebook, glance at the doodles: little flower still intact, sunflower still leaning, ecosystem undisturbed. I add one last sketch—a tiny battery icon at 2%—and caption it “Social Power Remaining.” Tomorrow I’ll recharge, rewrite, rehearse. Maybe upgrade to 3%. Maybe slip a real compliment into audible space. For now, I tuck the notebook against my chest like a shield, navigate the hallway crowds, and let my articulate inner voice whisper the lines I’ll try again in the morning, when the bell resets everything and the sunflower tilts back my way.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates exceptional adherence to the prompt's requirements, delivering a compelling first-person narrative that captures the authentic voice of an intelligent 8th-grade girl with social anxiety. The author skillfully constructs the internal monologue format, creating a believable stream of consciousness that reveals the protagonist's articulate inner voice contrasted against her verbal struggles. The characterization is particularly strong. Sadie emerges as a fully realized character whose intelligence manifests through her sophisticated vocabulary and observational skills, while her anxiety creates realistic barriers to expression. Her cataloging of details about Evan ("the way the right corner of his mouth lifts before he laughs, like a comma waiting for the next clause") feels authentic to both her age and personality type. The supporting characters, while briefly sketched, serve their purpose effectively as "safety buffers" and group dynamics. The prose demonstrates considerable elegance and creativity. Metaphors like "my stomach flips so hard I half-expect it to knock over the graduated cylinders" and "freezing up like an overheated laptop" feel natural to the character's tech-savvy generation while avoiding forced contemporaneity. The recurring motif of rehearsal tracks and response scenarios effectively illustrates the exhausting mental gymnastics of social anxiety. The imagery is consistently strong without becoming overwrought. The botanical sketches serve as both character development and metaphor, while technical details like "spectrophotometric" demonstrate Sadie's academic prowess without feeling forced. The author maintains excellent balance between showing Sadie's internal complexity and her external struggles. The emotional engagement is high throughout. Readers can viscerally feel Sadie's anxiety, from the physical sensations ("my pulse jumps so high I swear it taps the underside of my chin") to the mental spiraling. The piece captures the universal experience of adolescent crushes while remaining specific to this character's particular struggles. The dialogue, though minimal by design, feels authentic when it occurs. Sadie's verbal stumbles ("See—later!") contrast effectively with her internal eloquence, and other characters speak naturally for their age group. The ending strikes an appropriate tone—neither falsely optimistic nor unnecessarily dark. Sadie's exhaustion is palpable, but her determination to "recharge, rewrite, rehearse" feels earned and realistic for her character. The final image of the notebook as shield and the promise to "try again in the morning" provides closure without suggesting unrealistic transformation. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphors, while creative, border on being slightly too clever for the character's immediate emotional state. However, these instances are rare and don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. The piece succeeds remarkably in its ambitious goal of sustaining reader interest through almost pure internal monologue while maintaining narrative momentum and emotional authenticity. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 17 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 2 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
